# Next.js Debugging & Insight Tools — Reference

> Based on Next.js 16.2 · App Router · React · March 2026

> ⚠️ **Dev ≠ Production.** Performance numbers measured in `next dev` are meaningless — React runs in development mode (extra checks, no minification, no tree-shaking). Use dev tools to debug **logic and structure**, not to benchmark performance. For real performance data, use production instrumentation (Phase 4).

> \* Entries marked with an asterisk were added or updated in Next.js 16.2.

## Overview

| Phase                 | Command                         | What you're debugging                                                        |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **1 — Development**   | `next dev`                      | Logic, routing, component state, server errors, fetch calls                  |
| **2 — Build Process** | `next build`                    | What routes were generated, why pages are dynamic, what params were resolved |
| **3 — Build Output**  | `npx next experimental-analyze` | Bundle size, what's in your JS chunks                                        |
| **4 — Production**    | `next start` / deployed         | Real performance, real Web Vitals, server-side tracing                       |

## 0 — Official Skill Sets

Before the phase-by-phase tool list, it helps to separate three layers that are easy to mix together.

### What layer they belong to

| Layer                     | What it does                                                                  | Examples                                                     |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Docs / knowledge**      | Gives the agent version-matched facts and API guidance                        | Bundled Next.js docs, `AGENTS.md`                            |
| **Skills / workflows**    | Gives the agent reusable playbooks for debugging, migration, and optimization | `next-skills`, `next-browser`, `vercel-react-best-practices` |
| **Live inspection tools** | Gives the agent evidence from the currently running app                       | Next.js DevTools MCP, Chrome DevTools MCP, React DevTools    |

The three official skill sets that matter here are:

- [`next-skills`](https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/next-skills)
- [`next-browser`](https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/next-browser)
- [`vercel-react-best-practices`](https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/agent-skills/vercel-react-best-practices)

These are **not all debugging tools in the same sense**. Two of them are mainly reusable guidance for agents, while one of them is an actual live inspection skill.

### What each set is for

| Set                               | What it is                                                                                                  | Best use in this reference                                                                                               | Live debugging?                                      |
| --------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| **`next-skills`**                 | A collection of Next.js-specific guidance skills                                                            | Helps an agent reason about routing, RSC boundaries, caching, error handling, and upgrades                               | **Indirect** — guidance, not runtime inspection      |
| **`next-browser`**                | An experimental skill that lets an agent inspect a running Next.js app through a persistent browser session | Best for development-time inspection of React trees, PPR shells, logs, network activity, and screenshots                 | **Direct** — dev-time inspection                     |
| **`vercel-react-best-practices`** | A React and Next.js performance/refactoring guide from Vercel                                               | Best for interpreting symptoms and choosing fixes for waterfalls, bundle size, rerenders, hydration, and rendering costs | **Indirect** — optimization and remediation guidance |

Inside **`next-skills`**, the most relevant entries for this document are:

| Skill                       | Main job                                                                                                                         | Where it matters most                                                    |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`next-best-practices`**   | Broad Next.js correctness guidance: file conventions, RSC boundaries, async APIs, error handling, hydration, and debug tricks    | Development and build-process debugging                                  |
| **`next-cache-components`** | Cache Components and PPR guidance: `use cache`, `cacheLife`, `cacheTag`, `updateTag`, dynamic holes, and static shell boundaries | Development and build-process debugging for cache behavior and streaming |
| **`next-upgrade`**          | Upgrade and codemod workflow for framework migrations                                                                            | Cases where the "bug" is really an upgrade mismatch or breaking change   |

The `next-skills` listing may show additional entries, but the three above are the ones with the clearest debugging, build, and migration relevance for this reference.

### Phase-by-phase fit

| Set                               | Development: Server                                                                                    | Development: Browser                                                                       | Build Process                  | Build Output                                 | Production                                                                     |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **`next-skills`**                 | **Strong guidance** for routing, server actions, async APIs, caching, and error handling               | **Useful guidance** for hydration, client boundaries, and Suspense behavior                | **Strong**                     | **Moderate**                                 | **Limited** — helps choose fixes, but does not observe production              |
| **`next-browser`**                | **Partial** — can surface dev-server errors, logs, and framework diagnostics through the browser skill | **Strongest fit** — DOM, network, screenshots, React tree, PPR shell                       | **No** — not a build-time tool | **No**                                       | **No** — dev-only                                                              |
| **`vercel-react-best-practices`** | **Strong guidance** for waterfalls, server fetching, caching, and Server Action patterns               | **Strong guidance** for rerenders, hydration, rendering behavior, and client data fetching | **Moderate**                   | **Strong** for bundle and rendering analysis | **Moderate** — useful for interpreting perf symptoms, not collecting telemetry |

Working mental model:

- Use **bundled docs / `AGENTS.md`** when you need the authoritative Next.js API or behavior.
- Use **live tools** like MCP, Chrome DevTools MCP, React DevTools, or `next-browser` when you need evidence from the running app.
- Use **`next-skills`** and **`vercel-react-best-practices`** after you have a symptom and need a likely explanation or a fix strategy.

---

## Phase 1 — Development (`next dev`)

**Server & Terminal tools** — output goes to the CLI terminal

---

### Default Log Routing

No flags or config needed. This is how Next.js routes output by default:

| Log type                                                                              | Where it appears |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------- |
| Server Component logs / errors                                                        | CLI terminal     |
| Client Component logs / errors                                                        | Browser console  |
| Fetch logs (with `logging.fetches`)                                                   | CLI terminal     |
| Browser warnings / errors (forwarded by default \* — see `logging.browserToTerminal`) | CLI terminal too |

**Important:** this table describes the **live `next dev` terminal** and the **browser console**. It does **not** describe the aggregate log file returned by Next.js DevTools MCP's `get_logs`. That file (`.next/dev/logs/next-development.log`) is a broader structured capture that can contain both **server output** and **browser console entries** side by side.

In other words:

- **Live terminal** = what the running `next dev` process prints
- **Browser console** = what the browser devtools show
- **MCP dev log file** = a combined development record used by agents / tooling

> **Docs:** [Debugging guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/debugging) · [Local development](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/local-development)

---

### `logging.fetches`

Logs every `fetch()` call to the **terminal** during `next dev` **and** `next build`. Shows URL, method, cache status.

```js
// next.config.js
logging: {
  fetches: {
    fullUrl: true,   // show full URL (not truncated)
  },
},
```

> **Docs:** [`logging` config](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/logging)

---

### `logging.browserToTerminal` \*

Browser **warnings and errors** are forwarded to the terminal by default in 16.2 — no config needed. Control the level with `logging.browserToTerminal` in `next.config.js`:

```js
// next.config.js
logging: {
  browserToTerminal: 'warn',  // default — warnings + errors
  // 'error' — errors only
  // true    — all console output (console.log, etc.)
  // false   — disable entirely
},
```

> Previously `browserDebugInfoInTerminal: true` (16.1 and earlier). That key no longer exists.

> **Docs:** [`logging` config](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/logging)

---

### How to read Next.js dev log badges \*

In development, Next.js may decorate log output with badges such as **`Prerender`**, **`Cache`**, or **`Server`** when it presents logs in browser tooling or structured dev logs.

These badges are **not part of your `console.log()` string**. They are metadata Next.js adds to help explain the context of the log record:

- **`Prerender`** — the log happened while rendering prerendered / static work
- **`Cache`** — the log came from cached work or a cached render path
- **`Server`** — the log originated on the server

This can be surprising because a **server-originated** log may still appear in the **browser console during development**. That does **not** mean the browser executed the same server code. It means one dev-time event is being surfaced in more than one debugging view.

Useful mental model:

- **Origin** = where the log was produced
- **Surface** = where Next.js shows it to you

So a log can have:

- **origin:** server
- **surfaces:** terminal, browser tooling, structured MCP dev log

By contrast, request timing lines such as `GET / 200 in 300ms (...)` are standard **server request logs**.

This mirrored / decorated behavior is primarily a **development-mode convenience**. You generally should not expect the same badges or mirrored visibility on a deployed production site.

> **Observed in practice:** on a live `next dev` server, server-render logs like `ROOT LAYOUT` or `POSTTTTTTTT` may appear with badges such as `Prerender` or `Cache` in browser tooling while still being recorded as `source: "Server"` in the structured dev log.

---

### `next dev --inspect`

Enables the **Node.js V8 inspector** so you can attach a debugger (Chrome DevTools, VS Code breakpoints, etc.) to the running server process and inspect server-side code.

```bash
next dev --inspect
```

- Opens a WebSocket on `ws://127.0.0.1:9229` by default
- Attach in Chrome via `chrome://inspect`
- Attach in VS Code via a `launch.json` entry with `"request": "attach"`
- Server-side breakpoint debugging only — not bundle analysis, not logs
- Also available for production: `next start --inspect` \* (see Phase 4)

> **Docs:** [Debugging guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/debugging#server-side-code)
> **Introduced in:** [Next.js 16.1 blog post](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1#easier-debugging-with-next-dev---inspect)

---

### Server Function Logging \*

During `next dev`, Next.js automatically logs **Server Function** (Server Action) execution to the terminal. Each log shows the function name, its arguments, execution time, and the source file it's defined in.

No config required — enabled by default in development.

> **Docs:** [Debugging guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/debugging)

---

### Dev Server Lock File \*

Next.js writes the running dev server's PID, port, and URL into a `.next/dev/lock` file. When a second `next dev` process starts in the same directory, it reads the lock file and prints an actionable error:

```
Error: Another next dev server is already running.
- Local:   http://localhost:3000
- PID:     12345
- Dir:     /path/to/project
- Log:     .next/dev/logs/next-development.log
Run kill 12345 to stop it.
```

Especially useful for AI coding agents, which frequently try to start `next dev` without checking if one is already running. The structured output gives the agent a PID to kill or a URL to connect to. Also prevents two `next build` processes from running simultaneously.

No config required — behavior is automatic.

> **Docs:** [16.2 AI improvements — Dev Server Lock File](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai#dev-server-lock-file)

---

**Browser & Dev Overlay tools** — output goes to the browser error overlay

---

### Hydration Diff Indicator \*

When a hydration mismatch occurs, the error overlay now clearly labels which content came from the server and which came from the client, using a `+ Client` / `- Server` legend.

No setup needed — visible in the dev error overlay when a mismatch occurs.

> **Docs:** [Debugging guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/debugging)

---

### Error Causes in Dev Overlay \*

The error overlay now displays full `Error.cause` chains, up to 5 levels deep. When an error wraps another error, all causes are shown in a flat list below the top-level error.

No setup needed — visible automatically in the dev error overlay.

> **Docs:** [Debugging guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/debugging)

---

### React DevTools — Three Separate Tools

The "React DevTools" label covers **three distinct tools** that work independently:

#### 1. Components Tab (browser extension)

Inspect the component tree, props, state, and hooks on any React site — yours or anyone else's. Works in **dev and production** (though component names may be minified in production).

No setup needed beyond installing the extension.

Install: [React DevTools on Chrome Web Store](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/react-developer-tools/fmkadmapgofadopljbjfkapdkoienihi)

#### 2. Profiler Tab (browser extension)

Record renders, see a flame graph, and rank components by render time. **Part of the same extension** as the Components tab.

| Environment            | Profiler tab                                              |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `next dev`             | ✅ Full profiling data                                    |
| `next build` (default) | ❌ Stripped by React for performance                      |
| `next build --profile` | ✅ Re-enabled (adds bundle overhead — temporary use only) |

#### 3. React Performance Tracks (Chrome Performance panel)

A **separate, built-in** feature in Chrome's Performance panel — no extension needed, and nothing to do with the Profiler tab above.

**Dev-only** (disabled in production). Just open Chrome DevTools → Performance → record a trace while `next dev` is running.

Adds React-specific lanes to the timeline:

| Track                 | What it shows                       |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| **Scheduler**         | When React scheduled work           |
| **Components**        | When individual components rendered |
| **Server Components** | RSC fetch/render timing             |
| **Server Requests**   | Network requests initiated by RSCs  |

> **Docs:** [React DevTools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) · [React Performance Tracks](https://react.dev/reference/dev-tools/react-performance-tracks)

---

**AI Agent tools** — split by where the signal comes from: server, browser, or both.

---

### Server-aware tooling

#### Next.js DevTools MCP

Exposes your running `next dev` server's internals to AI coding agents (like GitHub Copilot) via the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io).

> ⚠️ **Dev-only.** Connects to `next dev`. Has no access to production.

**Setup** — add to `.mcp.json` at the project root:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "next-devtools": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "next-devtools-mcp@latest"]
    }
  }
}
```

Then start `next dev`. The MCP server auto-discovers and connects to the running instance.

**What agents can query:**

| Tool                      | What it returns                                                  |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `get_errors`              | Build errors, runtime errors, type errors from the dev server    |
| `get_logs`                | Path to aggregate dev log file (browser console + server output) |
| `get_page_metadata`       | Routes, components, rendering mode for a specific page           |
| `get_project_metadata`    | Project structure, config, dev server URL                        |
| `get_server_action_by_id` | Source file + function name for a Server Action by ID            |

**Important:** `get_logs` is not just "whatever the terminal currently shows." In practice it points to a broader development log that can mix **`source: "Server"`** and **`source: "Browser"`** entries in one file.

> **Docs:** [MCP guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/mcp)

---

### Browser-aware tooling

#### Chrome DevTools MCP

Connects an AI agent directly to a **Chrome browser** instance — giving it access to the DOM, console, network requests, and screenshots.

> ⚠️ **Not Next.js-aware.** This is a generic browser automation bridge. It does not know about App Router, RSCs, or Server Actions. Useful for inspecting what the browser sees, not what the server did.

**Setup** — add to `.mcp.json`:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "chrome-devtools": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest"]
    }
  }
}
```

> **Docs:** [MCP guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/mcp)

---

### Hybrid tooling

#### `next-browser` (Experimental) \*

[@vercel/next-browser](https://github.com/vercel-labs/next-browser) is an experimental CLI that gives AI agents terminal access to a running Next.js app. It exposes browser-level data — screenshots, network requests, console logs — alongside framework-specific insights from React DevTools: component trees, props, hooks, PPR shell analysis, and errors. All returned as structured text via shell commands.

> ⚠️ **Experimental.** API is evolving. Dev-only — connects to `next dev`.

Install as a [skill](https://skills.sh/) and invoke with `/next-browser` in Claude Code, Cursor, or any agent that supports skills. The CLI manages a Chromium instance with React DevTools pre-loaded — no browser configuration required.

```bash
npx skills add vercel-labs/next-browser
```

**What it can do:**

| Command / capability      | What it returns                                                            |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `next-browser tree`       | React component tree — props, hooks, state, source-mapped file locations   |
| `next-browser ppr lock`   | PPR shell — shows only the static region prerendered at the edge           |
| `next-browser ppr unlock` | Diagnose what's blocking the static shell (fetch owner + file + next step) |
| `next-browser screenshot` | Visual capture of the current page                                         |
| Network monitoring        | Requests since last navigation, including Server Actions                   |
| Error / log access        | Build and runtime issues from the dev server                               |

Unlike [Next.js DevTools MCP](#nextjs-devtools-mcp) (server-only) and [Chrome DevTools MCP](#chrome-devtools-mcp) (generic browser), `next-browser` bridges both — Next.js-aware diagnostics as structured terminal text the agent can parse.

> **Docs:** [16.2 AI improvements — Experimental Agent DevTools](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai#experimental-agent-devtools)
> **Skill.sh docs:** [next-browser](https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/next-browser/next-browser)

---

## Phase 2 — Build Process (`next build`)

### `next build --debug`

Enables **verbose build output**. Prints the routing rules that Next.js applies silently by default:

- **Redirects** (including the auto-generated trailing-slash redirect)
- **Rewrites**
- **Headers**

```bash
next build --debug
```

| Section     | Normal build | `--debug` build |
| ----------- | ------------ | --------------- |
| Route table | ✅           | ✅              |
| Redirects   | ❌ hidden    | ✅ shown        |
| Rewrites    | ❌ hidden    | ✅ shown        |
| Headers     | ❌ hidden    | ✅ shown        |

**Example output:**

```
Redirects
┌ source: /:path+/
├ destination: /:path+
└ permanent: true
```

That trailing-slash redirect is auto-generated by Next.js whenever `trailingSlash` is not explicitly set — you'd never know it's there without `--debug`.

> **Docs:** [CLI reference](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next)

---

### `next build --debug-prerender`

Prints detailed prerender tracing — which pages were statically generated vs. dynamically rendered and why. Useful when a page is unexpectedly dynamic.

```bash
next build --debug-prerender
```

> **Docs:** [CLI reference](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next) · [Debugging guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/debugging)

---

### `next build --debug-build-paths=<path>`

Shows which dynamic params were generated for a specific route during `generateStaticParams`.

```bash
next build --debug-build-paths=/blog/[slug]
```

> **Docs:** [CLI reference](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next)

---

### `next build --profile`

Re-enables React profiling instrumentation in the production build. Lets the **React DevTools Profiler tab** work on a deployed site.

```bash
next build --profile
```

> **Temporary use only.** Adds overhead to production JS bundles. Build normally once debugging is done.

> **Docs:** [CLI reference](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next)

---

## Phase 3 — Build Output

### `npx next experimental-analyze`

Available in **Next.js 16.1+**. Runs the experimental **Next.js Bundle Analyzer** for Turbopack and opens an interactive view of your production bundle output. No config required — it reflects the actual production bundles that Next.js generates.

```bash
npx next experimental-analyze
```

Use this to find:

- Unexpectedly large dependencies
- Code that should have been tree-shaken but wasn't
- Client chunks that could be split or lazy-loaded
- Which route pulled a module into the bundle
- The full import chain showing why a module is included
- CSS, JSON, and other asset weight alongside JavaScript
- Differences between **client** and **server** bundle views

The interactive UI is especially useful because it is integrated with Turbopack's module graph. You can filter by route, environment, and asset type, then click a module to inspect its size and trace exactly where it entered the bundle.

If you want to compare before/after refactors or share results with someone else, you can also write the analysis to disk instead of only opening the interactive UI:

```bash
npx next experimental-analyze --output
```

That writes the report to `.next/diagnostics/analyze`, which is useful for diffing bundle changes across optimization passes.

> **Docs:** [Package bundling guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/package-bundling#nextjs-bundle-analyzer-experimental) · [CLI reference](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next)
> **Introduced in:** [Next.js 16.1 blog post](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1#nextjs-bundle-analyzer-experimental)

---

## Phase 4 — Production

> ⚠️ React DevTools Performance Tracks and Next.js DevTools MCP do **not** work in production. The tools below are what you have.

**Server tools** — output goes to the Node.js process or tracing backend

---

### `instrumentation.ts` + OpenTelemetry

Next.js has first-class support for [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) via `instrumentation.ts`. The right path for **server-side** production monitoring — traces, spans, custom metrics.

```ts
// instrumentation.ts  (project root)
export async function register() {
  if (process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME === 'nodejs') {
    await import('./otel-setup'); // your OTel SDK bootstrap
  }
}
```

> **Docs:** [Open Telemetry guide](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/open-telemetry) · [`instrumentation.ts`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/instrumentation)

---

### `next start --inspect` \*

Extends `--inspect` to the production server. Attach a Node.js debugger to a running `next start` process — useful for debugging issues that only reproduce in production, or for CPU/memory profiling of the real build.

```bash
next start --inspect
```

- Opens a WebSocket on `ws://127.0.0.1:9229` by default
- Attach in Chrome via `chrome://inspect`
- See [Chrome documentation](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/performance/nodejs) for Node.js profiling

> **Docs:** [CLI reference](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next)

---

**Browser tools** — output goes to browser DevTools

---

### `useReportWebVitals`

Built-in Next.js hook (`next/web-vitals`) — no plugin needed. Fires for every [Core Web Vital](https://web.dev/vitals/) (LCP, FID, CLS, etc.) **in production**. Forward the data to any analytics endpoint.

```tsx
// app/layout.tsx  (or a dedicated Client Component)
'use client';
import { useReportWebVitals } from 'next/web-vitals';

export function WebVitals() {
  useReportWebVitals((metric) => {
    console.log(metric); // or POST to your analytics endpoint
  });
}
```

> **Docs:** [`useReportWebVitals`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-report-web-vitals)

---

### React DevTools in Production

| Tool                     | Works in production?                                             |
| ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Components tab           | ✅ Works — but component names may be minified                   |
| Profiler tab             | ❌ Stripped by default — use `next build --profile` to re-enable |
| React Performance Tracks | ❌ Dev-only                                                      |

The Components tab is useful for **client-side component inspection** even in production. Server Components are not visible — they only exist on the server.

> **Docs:** [React DevTools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools)

---

### Generic Browser Tools

Standard browser DevTools work in production but have **no Next.js awareness**:

| Tool            | What it sees                                 |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Network tab     | HTTP requests (no RSC/Server Action context) |
| Performance tab | Paint, layout, scripting timings             |
| Lighthouse      | Accessibility, SEO, Core Web Vitals audit    |

These are useful for general HTTP/rendering profiling but cannot tell you anything about App Router internals, caching, or Server Components.

---

## Suggested Project Setup

Recommended `package.json` scripts:

```json
"scripts": {
  "dev": "next dev",
  "dev:inspect": "next dev --inspect",
  "debug": "next build --debug",
  "build": "next build",
  "start": "next start",
  "start:inspect": "next start --inspect"
}
```

Recommended `next.config.js` additions for development:

```js
logging: {
  fetches: { fullUrl: true },          // log fetch URLs + cache status to terminal
  // browserToTerminal: 'warn',        // default — browser warnings + errors already forwarded *
  // browserToTerminal: true,          // uncomment to forward ALL browser console output *
},
```

Recommended `.mcp.json` at project root:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "next-devtools": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "next-devtools-mcp@latest"]
    },
    "chrome-devtools": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest"]
    }
  }
}
```

---

## Quick Decision Guide

> "What tool do I reach for?"

| Goal                                                | Phase        | Tool                                               |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Set a breakpoint in server code                     | Dev          | `next dev --inspect` + attach debugger             |
| Inspect component tree / props / state              | Dev          | React DevTools → Components tab                    |
| Profile renders, find slow components               | Dev          | React DevTools → Profiler tab                      |
| See React render timing in a flame graph            | Dev          | Chrome Performance panel → React tracks            |
| See what fetch calls are made server-side           | Dev          | `logging.fetches` in `next.config.js`              |
| Browser warnings/errors in terminal (default) \*    | Dev          | automatic — no config needed                       |
| Forward all browser console to terminal \*          | Dev          | `logging.browserToTerminal: true` in next.config   |
| Explain why the same log appears in multiple places | Dev          | check origin (`Server` / `Browser`) vs surface     |
| Debug Server Function execution \*                  | Dev          | automatic terminal logging — no config needed      |
| Debug hydration mismatches \*                       | Dev          | dev error overlay — `+ Client` / `- Server` diff   |
| Trace chained error causes \*                       | Dev          | dev error overlay — `Error.cause` chain (5 levels) |
| Ask AI agent about errors / routes / Server Actions | Dev          | Next.js DevTools MCP                               |
| Let AI agent inspect the browser DOM / console      | Dev          | Chrome DevTools MCP                                |
| Let AI agent inspect React tree / PPR shell \*      | Dev          | `next-browser` (experimental skill)                |
| Prevent/detect duplicate `next dev` process \*      | Dev          | Dev Server Lock File (automatic)                   |
| See what redirects/rewrites are active              | Build        | `next build --debug`                               |
| See why a page is dynamic not static                | Build        | `next build --debug-prerender`                     |
| Trace which params were generated                   | Build        | `next build --debug-build-paths=/route/[param]`    |
| Analyze bundle size                                 | Build output | `npx next experimental-analyze`                    |
| Server-side tracing / performance spans             | Production   | `instrumentation.ts` + OpenTelemetry               |
| Client-side Core Web Vitals                         | Production   | `useReportWebVitals` (built-in, no plugin)         |
| Use React Profiler on a live site (temporarily)     | Production   | `next build --profile` (adds bundle overhead)      |
| Attach debugger to production server \*             | Production   | `next start --inspect`                             |
| General HTTP / paint / SEO audit                    | Production   | Browser DevTools: Network, Performance, Lighthouse |
